Hidden messages.<[silence:600ms]> Unbreakable locks.<[silence:600ms]>

The word cryptography comes from Greek kryptós ("hidden") and gráphein ("to write").<[silence:600ms]> It’s the art of turning plain words into puzzles only the right key can solve—like a letter written in invisible ink that only appears when heated.<[silence:600ms]>

Before computers, spies used tools like the Spartan scytale:<[silence:200ms]> a wooden rod wrapped with a strip of parchment.<[silence:600ms]> The message made sense only when rewound around an identical rod.<[silence:600ms]> Wrong rod?<[silence:200ms]> Gibberish.<[silence:600ms]> In World War II, the Enigma machine scrambled letters using rotating wheels;<[silence:200ms]> breaking it cut the war short by years.<[silence:600ms]>

Today, cryptography secures every password, bank transfer, and private chat.<[silence:600ms]> Your phone’s AES-256 encryption turns "hello" into a 256-bit code like:<[silence:200ms]>
`2b7e151628aed2a6abf7158809cf4f3c`
—unlockable only with your passcode.<[silence:600ms]> Even supercomputers would take 36.7 quintillion years to guess it.<[silence:600ms]>

clean_story:
Hidden messages. Unbreakable locks.

The word cryptography comes from Greek kryptós ("hidden") and gráphein ("to write"). It’s the art of turning plain words into puzzles only the right key can solve—like a letter written in invisible ink that only appears when heated.

Before computers, spies used tools like the Spartan scytale: a wooden rod wrapped with a strip of parchment. The message made sense only when rewound around an identical rod. Wrong rod? Gibberish. In World War II, the Enigma machine scrambled letters using rotating wheels; breaking it cut the war short by years.

Today, cryptography secures every password, bank transfer, and private chat. Your phone’s AES-256 encryption turns "hello" into a 256-bit code like:
`2b7e151628aed2a6abf7158809cf4f3c`
—unlockable only with your passcode. Even supercomputers would take 36.7 quintillion years to guess it.

Three related subjects are quantum encryption, steganography techniques, and how codes shaped wars.
